title: Assistant-Guided Decoding
tldr: A small model drafts tokens; a larger model verifies them to speed up inference without sacrificing quality.
plain_english: Normally, an LLM generates one token at a time, which is slow. Assistant-guided decoding uses a faster, smaller model to predict the next few tokens, then has the main model check them all at once. If the main model agrees, you've generated multiple tokens in nearly the same time as one—like using a spotter in weightlifting to handle extra reps safely.
how_it_works: During the prefill phase, both models process the prompt. In the decode phase, the assistant generates k candidate tokens. The target model verifies them in parallel using its KV cache. Tokens the target model rejects are discarded; accepted tokens move forward. This trades one small-model pass for k target-model forward passes run in a single batch, reducing total wall-clock time. Common architectures include external draft models (classical speculative decoding), fused decode heads (Medusa), and dedicated speculator heads (EAGLE).
why_it_matters: Token-by-token generation is the bottleneck in LLM inference latency. Assistant-guided decoding cuts time-to-first-token and token-generation latency by 2–4x in production, making interactive applications viable. Most managed APIs and production deployments now use it.
example: A voice assistant using Llama 70B with Medusa heads can generate 50 tokens in roughly the time the base model alone would take to generate 15–20. An inference provider like vLLM automatically enables this; users see lower p99 latency without changing their code.
pitfalls: Rejection sampling occurs when the target disagrees with the assistant; too many rejections waste compute and negate speedup—tune assistant model capacity carefully.
The assistant must be significantly faster but reasonably accurate; a random guesser wastes cycles; a model that's too weak provides no benefit.
KV cache overhead grows with longer sequences; assistant-guided decoding works best when the target's cache-hit ratio is high (short to medium contexts).
Merging or retraining the assistant head can make the target model's weights incompatible; version management across model updates is non-trivial.
when_use: Use assistant-guided decoding when you need sub-second latency for interactive workloads, have enough memory for a small auxiliary model, and the target model is compute-bound rather than memory-bound.
when_avoid: Avoid it if memory is extremely constrained, latency requirements are relaxed (batch inference), or you're optimizing for maximum throughput rather than minimum latency.

faq.q: What is the difference between assistant-guided decoding and speculative decoding?
faq.a: Assistant-guided decoding is the umbrella term for all speculative methods using one or more auxiliary models to propose tokens. Classical speculative decoding is one implementation; Medusa (fused heads) and EAGLE (dedicated speculators) are others.

faq.q: How much faster is assistant-guided decoding?
faq.a: Typical speedups are 1.5x to 4x on latency, depending on acceptance rate, context length, and hardware. Longer sequences see lower speedups because the target's KV cache grows and memory bandwidth becomes the bottleneck.

faq.q: Can I use assistant-guided decoding with any model?
faq.a: You need either a separate small model, a model with built-in decode heads (Medusa/EAGLE), or the ability to train an auxiliary head. Standard Llama or Mistral models require you to pair them with a draft model or fork to add speculator heads.

faq.q: What happens if the small model's prediction is wrong?
faq.a: The target model rejects it during verification, and decoding resumes from the last accepted token. This is called rejection sampling and is correct by design, but high rejection rates hurt speedup.

faq.q: Does assistant-guided decoding change the output quality?
faq.a: No. The target model always verifies, so output quality is identical to standard decoding. The only variable is speed.

title: Beam Search
tldr: Decoding method that tracks multiple top candidate sequences in parallel, returning the highest-probability complete sequence.
plain_english: Instead of committing to one word at a time, beam search keeps B parallel "candidates" alive and expands all of them together. At each step, it scores all new possibilities, picks the B best overall, and repeats until all candidates finish. Think of it like exploring multiple paths through a maze simultaneously and picking the one that led to the best exit.
how_it_works: You set a beam width B (typically 4–10). Start with B copies of your input. At each generation step, expand every beam by the vocabulary size, score all results, and keep only the top B by cumulative probability. Continue until all beams output an end token. Return the sequence with the highest joint probability. Unlike greedy decoding, which locks in the highest-scoring word instantly, beam search explores alternatives. Unlike sampling, it's deterministic—same input always yields the same output.
why_it_matters: Beam search was the standard for neural machine translation and still powers production systems where output quality (perplexity, exact match) matters more than diversity. Modern chat models rarely use it because (a) wide beams waste compute on large language models and (b) the safer, more repetitive outputs it produces don't match user expectations for conversation.
example: An NMT system translating English to French with B=5: at each token position, it explores the 5 most-likely partial translations. A 10-word input generates roughly 10 generation steps × 5 beams = 50 candidate paths evaluated in parallel. After all beams finish, you get back the single translation with highest probability under the model. This outperforms greedy (B=1) but uses 5× more memory.
pitfalls: Increasing beam width beyond B=5–10 often degrades output quality because model likelihood estimates become unreliable, causing all beams to converge on low-entropy, repetitive text.
Beam search is deterministic but not reproducible across different model implementations or tokenizers; log-probabilities can vary slightly.
Very long sequences cause beam width to appear ineffective: models lose coherence independently of B, so wider beams don't recover quality.
Combining beam search with length penalties or n-gram blocking can produce unexpected interactions—tune together, not separately.
when_use: Use beam search for machine translation, abstractive summarization, or structured generation tasks where you need one high-confidence output and can afford B parallel forward passes.
when_avoid: Avoid beam search for chat and open-ended text generation where diversity is expected, or for models so large that B=5 doubles latency unacceptably.

faq.q: Is beam search the same as greedy decoding?
faq.a: No. Greedy decoding picks the single highest-probability token at each step and never backtracks. Beam search tracks B candidates in parallel, allowing it to explore alternatives and recover from early mistakes. Beam search typically achieves lower perplexity but is slower.

faq.q: Why doesn't beam search work well for ChatGPT-style models?
faq.a: Modern LLMs are too large to run B=5 beams efficiently, and beam search produces safer, more repetitive outputs. Users expect varied, conversational responses, not the single highest-probability completion.

faq.q: What happens if I set beam width very high, like B=50?
faq.a: Quality often drops because the model's probability estimates become unreliable. All 50 beams tend to converge on the same low-entropy, repetitive outputs. B=5–10 is typically optimal.

faq.q: Does beam search guarantee the optimal translation?
faq.a: No. It returns the sequence with highest joint probability under the model, but the model itself may assign high probability to suboptimal translations. Beam search respects the model; it doesn't fix poor model training.

faq.q: How much slower is beam search than greedy decoding?
faq.a: Roughly B times slower on the same hardware, since you compute B forward passes per step. A B=5 beam on a model with 2ms per-token latency costs ~10ms per token instead of 2ms.

title: Constrained Decoding
tldr: Generation that masks disallowed tokens at each step to guarantee output matches a formal grammar, regex, or JSON schema.
plain_english: Instead of letting a model pick any token at each step, constrained decoding computes which tokens are still valid given what you've already generated and your constraints (like a JSON schema), and blocks everything else. Think of it like autocomplete that only suggests words that keep your sentence grammatically correct. The result is 100% structured output—no malformed JSON, no regex violations.
how_it_works: At each generation step, the decoder runs your partial output against the constraint grammar (JSON schema, regex, or BNF). It builds a set of valid next tokens—only those that don't violate the grammar. Before sampling, it masks the logits of all other tokens to zero probability, forcing the model to pick from the safe set. Libraries like Outlines and lm-format-enforcer do this masking. The process repeats until generation ends or you hit a valid terminal state. This is stricter than logit bias or JSON mode because invalid continuations become mathematically impossible, not just unlikely.
why_it_matters: Tool calls, API responses, and data pipelines need bulletproof structure. Constrained decoding turns probabilistic generation into a guarantee: your JSON is always valid, your regex always matches, your function calls always parse. This eliminates post-generation validation and retry loops that slow down agentic workflows.
example: An agent building SQL queries uses constrained decoding with a BNF grammar for valid SQL syntax. Without it, the model generates malformed queries 5–10% of the time, requiring re-parsing and retry. With constraints, every generated query is syntactically correct on the first pass, reducing latency and error handling overhead.
pitfalls: Constraining to an overly strict schema may cut off valid reasoning paths; the model must stay valid at every intermediate step, not just the end state.
Grammar compilation at runtime is expensive; cache or pre-compile grammars in production.
Complex nested schemas (deeply recursive JSON) can cause token masking to become a bottleneck if the valid token set shrinks too small at each step.
Not all tokenizers play well with every grammar; whitespace handling and token boundaries can trip up regex or BNF rules.
when_use: Use constrained decoding when you need 100% structural validity (JSON API responses, SQL queries, function calls), or when post-generation validation is too slow or unreliable.
when_avoid: Avoid when the constraint is so tight it forces unnatural token sequences, or when latency of grammar checking matters more than correctness, or when the output is prose that benefits from fluency over rigid structure.

faq.q: Is constrained decoding the same as JSON mode?
faq.a: No. JSON mode guides the model to try to output JSON but doesn't guarantee it. Constrained decoding masks invalid tokens so JSON is mathematically certain—no malformed brackets or escaped quotes.

faq.q: Does constrained decoding slow down generation?
faq.a: Yes, slightly. Grammar checking and token masking add overhead per step. For short outputs or simple grammars it's negligible; for long outputs or complex BNF rules, you may see 10–30% latency increase.

faq.q: Can I use constrained decoding with any LLM?
faq.a: Any LLM with token-level access (via logits or an API that supports it). Ollama, llama.cpp, Outlines, and Guidance support it; some closed APIs like OpenAI do not expose the required hooks.

faq.q: What's the difference between regex and BNF constraints?
faq.a: Regex matches flat token sequences (e.g., phone numbers, ISO dates). BNF is a formal grammar that handles nested and recursive structures (e.g., JSON, code). Use regex for simple patterns, BNF for complex hierarchies.

faq.q: Does constrained decoding work with streaming?
faq.a: Yes, but grammar checking happens on each token as it's generated, so you cannot stream until the token is validated. Streaming latency is higher than unconstrained generation.

title: Contrastive Decoding
tldr: Subtract a small model's predictions from a large model's to suppress generic tokens and amplify expert reasoning.
plain_english: Contrastive decoding runs two models in parallel: a capable expert and a smaller, less sophisticated amateur. Instead of sampling directly from the expert, you sample from the difference between them—boosting tokens the expert likes but the amateur doesn't. This pushes the output away from generic patterns toward more distinctive, factual reasoning.
how_it_works: At each generation step, you compute logits from both the expert and amateur models. You then calculate a weighted difference: log(p_expert) − λ · log(p_amateur), where λ controls how much you penalize amateur-preferred tokens. You sample from this adjusted distribution rather than from the expert alone. This requires two forward passes per token, roughly doubling inference cost. The amateur model is typically much smaller (e.g., a 7B vs. 70B expert), making the overhead manageable on modern hardware.
why_it_matters: This technique extracts better quality without retraining—useful when you have an expert model but want to reduce hallucination or generic output. It's a practical post-hoc adjustment with measurable gains on reasoning and factuality benchmarks, making it valuable for teams optimizing existing deployments.
example: A research team using a 70B model for mathematical problem-solving finds outputs are often verbose but shallow. By running a 7B model alongside and contrasting predictions, they push the 70B toward longer chains of reasoning and fewer formula-lookup mistakes. The doubled inference latency (50ms → 100ms) is acceptable for offline grading tasks.
pitfalls: Setting λ too high suppresses legitimate expert behavior; too low wastes the amateur signal. Tuning requires validation data.
Amateur model must be genuinely 'dumber' (lower loss, simpler architecture) or contrastive signal is noise.
Doubles inference cost and latency; not suitable for real-time, high-throughput settings without optimization.
Works best on factuality/reasoning tasks; gains diminish on creative or style-sensitive generation where generic diversity helps.
when_use: Use contrastive decoding when you own both an expert and smaller baseline model, have batch/offline generation time, and need to reduce hallucination or boost reasoning quality on factuality-sensitive tasks.
when_avoid: Avoid when you need sub-100ms latency, rely on a single model only, or prioritize creative/fluent generation over factual precision.

faq.q: Is contrastive decoding the same as speculative decoding?
faq.a: No. Speculative decoding uses a small model to draft tokens, then the large model verifies them—a speedup technique. Contrastive decoding uses logit subtraction to change output quality. They solve different problems.

faq.q: How much does contrastive decoding improve factuality?
faq.a: Gains vary by task and model pair. Li et al. (2022) reported 3–8% improvements on factuality benchmarks, but real gains depend on how well the amateur model captures generic patterns you want to suppress.

faq.q: Can I use any small model as the amateur?
faq.a: Ideally, yes—but it should be noticeably weaker (lower perplexity, fewer parameters) and trained on similar data. Using a random or unrelated model produces poor contrastive signal.

faq.q: What value of lambda should I use?
faq.a: Start with λ = 0.5–1.0 and validate on a held-out set. Higher λ (2+) increases contrast but risks erasing valid expert predictions. No universal default exists.

faq.q: Does contrastive decoding work for non-English languages?
faq.a: Yes, the mechanism is language-agnostic. Effectiveness depends on whether both models were trained on the target language and whether generic patterns are similar across languages.

title: Deterministic Decoding (Seeded Generation)
tldr: Set temperature to 0 or use a fixed seed to produce identical outputs for the same input across runs.
plain_english: When you want your model to always give the same answer to the same question, you turn off randomness in token selection. Think of it like switching a dice game to a rule-based one: instead of rolling for the next word, you always pick the most likely word. This matters for tests and reproducible research.
how_it_works: At temperature=0, the model picks the token with the highest probability at each step (greedy decoding). With APIs that support seed parameters, you lock the random number generator to a fixed starting point, so stochastic sampling produces identical sequences. The catch: floating-point arithmetic order varies across hardware and library versions, so bit-for-bit reproducibility across providers is not guaranteed. Set temperature to 0 for pure determinism on a single system; use seeds when you need reproducible randomness across runs.
why_it_matters: Deterministic outputs let you write regression tests that catch model drift, reproduce research results exactly, and guarantee consistency in production systems where users expect the same answer every time. Without it, A/B testing becomes confusing and debugging model behavior becomes impossible.
example: A regulatory compliance team needs identical model outputs for audit trails. They set temperature=0 when generating explanations for loan denials so that the same applicant profile always produces the same explanation text. This lets them version-control their model behavior and prove consistency to auditors.
pitfalls: Reproducibility across cloud providers fails even with seeds because internal serving optimizations and hardware differences cause floating-point drift.
Temperature=0 eliminates creativity; use it only when you need identical output, not when variety helps—like brainstorming or content generation.
Confusing seed reproducibility with true determinism; a seed gives you the same sequence within one run, but not across different library versions or accelerators.
when_use: Use deterministic decoding when you need identical outputs for regression testing, audit trails, reproducible research, or any production system where consistency across runs matters more than diversity.
when_avoid: Avoid when you want varied or creative outputs—like summarization, ideation, or open-ended chat—or when reproducibility across different hardware or cloud providers is critical.

faq.q: Is temperature 0 the same as deterministic decoding?
faq.a: Temperature=0 is one way to achieve determinism (greedy decoding), but it's not the only way. APIs may also offer a seed parameter for reproducible stochastic sampling. Both produce identical outputs for the same input within one system, but temperature=0 removes randomness entirely.

faq.q: Will my seeded model outputs be identical across different cloud providers?
faq.a: No. Even with the same seed, floating-point arithmetic order and internal optimizations differ between providers, causing drift. For cross-provider reproducibility, you need bit-for-bit identical inference code and hardware.

faq.q: Can I use deterministic decoding for creative tasks?
faq.a: You can, but it's not ideal. Temperature=0 always picks the most likely token, which works for factual tasks but produces repetitive, dull output for creative writing, brainstorming, or open-ended generation.

faq.q: How do I implement seeded generation in my API calls?
faq.a: Most modern APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) accept a seed parameter in the request body. Pass temperature=0 or your seed value depending on the provider's API. Check their documentation for the exact parameter name and behavior.

faq.q: Why does my model give different outputs with the same seed across software versions?
faq.a: Library updates, tokenizer changes, or hardware acceleration differences can alter floating-point reduction order or probability calculations, breaking seed reproducibility. Version lock your dependencies if byte-exact reproducibility is critical.

title: Diverse Beam Search
tldr: Beam search variant that penalizes similar candidates so you get N meaningfully different outputs instead of near-duplicates.
plain_english: Standard beam search returns N candidates that often differ by just one or two tokens—essentially the same output repeated. Diverse beam search fixes this by blocking tokens that earlier groups already chose, forcing the model to explore genuinely different directions. Think of it as telling a brainstorm group: 'Don't just rephrase what the first person said; give me actually different ideas.'
how_it_works: Diverse beam search partitions your N beams into groups. Each group generates the next token independently, but before a group selects its token, it subtracts a penalty score based on tokens chosen by groups ranked higher. This penalty is weighted—earlier groups influence later ones more strongly. The penalty function typically counts how many times a token was already selected, scaled by a diversity parameter you control. Result: beams diverge early, producing candidates that differ in semantic or structural ways, not just surface tokens.
why_it_matters: Beam search alone gives you a false sense of choice—you think you have N options when you really have 1 option plus N-1 typos. Diverse beam search turns decoding into a real multi-hypothesis tool. You get distinct paraphrases, multiple reasoning paths, or alternative phrasings for the same meaning. This matters for brainstorming, query refinement, and QA systems where you need to surface legitimate alternatives, not noise.
example: A documentation chatbot generates 3 alternative explanations of the same API endpoint. With vanilla beam search, all three say roughly 'call POST /api/users with JSON body.' With diverse beam search, one explains the endpoint procedurally, one shows a cURL example, and one emphasizes error codes—all correct, all genuinely different. This forces the beam search to explore different token sequences early, preventing collapse to a single mode.
pitfalls: Setting diversity penalty too high makes outputs incoherent—early beams get good samples, but later beams are forced so far into unexplored territory they become grammatically broken.
Diverse beam search does not guarantee semantic diversity, only surface-token diversity—two outputs can paraphrase each other perfectly while still differing in which tokens the algorithm penalized.
Partitioning beams into groups is a design choice; poor grouping (e.g., too few groups) limits diversity gains since later groups still compete for the same token space.
Computational cost increases linearly with the diversity penalty calculation—each group must track penalty scores across all prior groups before decoding the next token.
when_use: Use diverse beam search when you need multiple genuinely different outputs (paraphrasing, multi-hypothesis QA, brainstorming), not just a ranked list of the same idea.
when_avoid: Avoid it when you want the single best output (greedy decoding is cheaper) or when you need strictly ranked candidates by probability (diversity breaks the confidence ordering).

faq.q: How is diverse beam search different from just running beam search multiple times?
faq.a: Running beam search multiple times gives you identical outputs (or near-identical, depending on tie-breaking). Diverse beam search modifies the decoding algorithm itself to steer beams apart during a single forward pass, guaranteeing structural difference without recomputation.

faq.q: Does diverse beam search work with all language models?
faq.a: Yes, it's a post-training decoding algorithm—no model retraining needed. It works with any autoregressive model. You control it via the diversity penalty hyperparameter at inference time.

faq.q: What's the diversity penalty parameter and how do I set it?
faq.a: It's a scalar (usually 0.0–1.0) that scales how much earlier beams penalize later beams' token choices. Start at 0.5; higher values force more divergence but risk incoherence. Lower values make groups compete more, reducing diversity.

faq.q: Can I use diverse beam search with large batch sizes?
faq.a: Yes, but the penalty calculation scales with the number of beams and groups. For large batches, compute becomes the bottleneck. Some frameworks like Hugging Face transformers optimize this, but verify latency before production use.

faq.q: Is diverse beam search the same as diverse decoding?
faq.a: Diverse beam search is one flavor of diverse decoding. The term 'diverse decoding' is broader and includes other methods like contrastive decoding or nucleus sampling variants.

acceptedAnswer.text: Diverse beam search is one flavor of diverse decoding. The term 'diverse decoding' is broader and includes other methods like contrastive decoding or nucleus sampling variants.

title: DRY Sampling
tldr: Repetition penalty that blocks repeated n-gram prefixes, not tokens, to reduce loops without suppressing common words.
plain_english: DRY sampling stops your model from repeating itself by tracking exact phrase patterns it's already said, then avoiding those patterns again. Unlike simpler repetition penalties that penalize any repeated word (which breaks natural language), DRY only blocks n-grams whose full prefix just appeared. This prevents the 'stuck in a loop' failure without flattening legitimate repetition.
how_it_works: During token generation, DRY maintains a window of recent n-grams (typically the last 64–128 tokens). When considering the next token, it checks: does this token complete an n-gram whose prefix already appeared in that window? If yes, it reduces the token's probability. If no, the token stays unpenalized. The penalty decays as you move further from the original occurrence, letting natural repetition resume after sufficient distance.
why_it_matters: Long-form generation (stories, dialogue, code) fails when models loop. Frequency penalty flattens common words like 'the' and breaks fluency. DRY fixes this by targeting pattern-level repetition instead of token-level, letting you generate thousands of tokens without deteriorating quality. It's the go-to for creative and technical text generation where both coherence and naturalness matter.
example: A creative writing tool using KoboldCpp with DRY sampling at window=64 can generate a 5,000-word story without entering loops like 'The hero walked to the hero walked to the hero.' With frequency penalty alone, the same tool would weaken 'the' and 'and' across the text, making sentences stiff. DRY keeps both words usable while blocking the exact repeated phrase pattern.
pitfalls: DRY requires tuning the n-gram window size; too small and it misses loops, too large and it over-penalizes legitimate repetition of common patterns.
It's not universally available—only Text Generation WebUI, KoboldCpp, and some inference engines expose it; most commercial APIs default to frequency or presence penalty.
DRY doesn't distinguish semantic similarity; it only blocks exact n-gram matches, so paraphrased repetition (same idea, different words) still happens.
Stacking DRY with other penalties (frequency, presence) can create unexpected interactions; test in isolation first.
when_use: Use DRY for creative generation (fiction, poetry, dialogue) or long-context tasks where you need both coherence and natural repetition, and you have access to an inference engine that supports it.
when_avoid: Avoid DRY when generating structured data, code with many required keywords, or when your inference framework doesn't expose the parameter—fall back to frequency penalty instead.

faq.q: How is DRY sampling different from frequency penalty?
faq.a: Frequency penalty penalizes any repeated token based on how often it's appeared overall; DRY only penalizes n-grams whose exact prefix just occurred. DRY preserves common words like 'the', while frequency penalty suppresses them globally.

faq.q: What window size should I use for DRY sampling?
faq.a: Start at 64–128 tokens. Larger windows catch more distant loops but may block legitimate patterns; smaller windows miss loops in long generation. Test with your output length and adjust based on whether you see unwanted repetition or over-penalization.

faq.q: Does DRY sampling work with code generation?
faq.a: Yes, but carefully. Code often repeats variable names and keywords intentionally. DRY prevents copy-paste errors but may block needed repeated patterns. Lower the penalty or window size, or use it alongside other constraints like grammar-guided generation.

faq.q: Can I combine DRY with temperature and top-p?
faq.a: Yes. DRY acts independently during decoding; temperature and top-p still control diversity. Combine them for best results: DRY blocks exact loops, temperature adds variation, top-p narrows the token pool. Start conservative and tune together.

faq.q: Is DRY sampling the same as reject sampling or beam search?
faq.a: No. DRY modifies probability during generation; reject sampling filters completed sequences afterward; beam search explores multiple paths in parallel. DRY is faster and more direct for loop prevention.

title: EAGLE Decoding
tldr: Lightweight auxiliary model predicts multiple next tokens from target LLM hidden states, speeding generation 3–4× with minimal quality loss.
plain_english: EAGLE attaches a small, trainable transformer head to your main LLM. Instead of generating one token at a time, this head looks at what the main model learned internally and guesses the next 2–4 tokens in parallel. Most guesses are wrong, but the main model checks them fast—like proofreading candidates instead of writing from scratch. You get 3–4× faster output with barely any quality drop.
how_it_works: EAGLE trains a compact auxiliary model (1–5% of your main LLM's parameters) on your target model's last-layer hidden states. At inference, after the main model emits one token, EAGLE predicts the next N tokens. The main model then validates these drafts in parallel via a single forward pass: it accepts correct predictions and stops at the first wrong one, resampling from that point. EAGLE-2 adds dynamic tree attention to explore multiple branches more efficiently. Both versions integrate directly into vLLM and TensorRT-LLM.
why_it_matters: Speculative decoding trades compute for latency. EAGLE achieves the best speed-quality tradeoff in production because its small head benefits from the main model's learned representations, producing far more accurate predictions than external draft models. This cuts time-to-completion for long outputs—critical for real-time chat, code generation, and retrieval workflows—without retraining the main model.
example: A team running Llama 2 70B for code completion sees 25-token outputs take 8 seconds baseline. After integrating EAGLE with vLLM, the same requests finish in ~2.2 seconds. The auxiliary model (5M params) trains for 4 hours on a single H100 using cached hidden states. Output quality stays identical; only latency improves.
pitfalls: EAGLE requires access to hidden states and must be trained per target model—you cannot reuse an EAGLE trained on Llama for Mistral.
Speedup degrades with very short sequences (< 5 tokens) because validation overhead dominates; benefits appear at ~20+ token generation.
Beam search and sampling with high temperature reduce draft accuracy; greedy or low-temperature decoding works best.
Memory footprint grows slightly; you load both the main model and auxiliary head, though the head is tiny.
when_use: Use EAGLE when you need sub-second latency for long outputs and can afford 4–8 hours of one-time training per model. Ideal for production chat, code, and retrieval-augmented generation.
when_avoid: Avoid EAGLE for short, real-time interactive loops (< 5 tokens), models you change weekly, or scenarios where training a new auxiliary per model is infeasible.

faq.q: What's the difference between EAGLE and Medusa?
faq.a: EAGLE uses the target model's hidden states to train its head, making predictions more accurate. Medusa trains heads on logits only. EAGLE typically achieves higher speedup with lower overhead.

faq.q: Does EAGLE work with quantized models?
faq.a: Yes, but the auxiliary head trains on hidden states before quantization. Quantization of the main model doesn't break EAGLE, though some speedup may be lost if quantization itself is fast.

faq.q: How long does it take to train EAGLE for a new model?
faq.a: 4–8 hours on a single H100 for a 70B model, using cached hidden states from a few thousand training examples. Smaller models train in 1–2 hours.

faq.q: Can I use EAGLE with batch inference?
faq.a: Yes. Continuous batching in vLLM handles EAGLE's parallel validation efficiently. Speedup multiplies across batches in production.

faq.q: Does EAGLE change the output tokens or just make generation faster?
faq.a: EAGLE does not change output quality. Speeds up the decoding process only; same tokens in the same order, just faster.

title: Entropy-Based Decoding
tldr: Decoding that adjusts sampling parameters per step based on model confidence, sampling broadly when uncertain and narrowly when confident.
plain_english: Instead of using one temperature setting for the entire generation, entropy-based decoding watches how 'sure' the model is at each step. When the model is uncertain (high entropy), it explores more options. When confident (low entropy), it locks in on obvious tokens. Think of it like cautiously trying many dishes when unsure what you want, then sticking with your favorite once you know.
how_it_works: At each generation step, compute the Shannon entropy of the token probability distribution. High entropy triggers wider sampling (higher temperature, higher top-p) to explore the possibility space. Low entropy triggers narrower sampling (lower temperature, greedy-like behavior) to avoid perturbing confident predictions. Some implementations also track entropy variance across steps to detect when the model oscillates between certainty and confusion, adjusting the adaptation rate accordingly.
why_it_matters: Fixed-temperature decoding forces a single exploration–exploitation tradeoff across all steps, wasting samples on trivial choices and over-constraining creative ones. Entropy-based decoding adapts on the fly, improving coherence in reasoning-heavy passages while preserving diversity in open-ended sections. This matters for long-context tasks where confidence varies dramatically.
example: A reasoning model solving a multi-step math problem exhibits high entropy when choosing a proof strategy (many valid approaches exist) and low entropy when selecting the next arithmetic operation. Entropy-based decoding automatically expands sampling at the strategy step and tightens it during computation, avoiding both over-exploration and premature convergence.
pitfalls: Computing entropy at every step adds latency; batch-friendly implementations are rare and slow.
Threshold-tuning for 'high' vs. 'low' entropy is model- and task-dependent; no universal preset exists.
Entropy alone ignores downstream consequences; high entropy in the current token can compound into incoherence later.
Most implementations assume well-calibrated models; out-of-distribution or adversarial inputs can produce misleading entropy signals.
when_use: Use entropy-based decoding for reasoning tasks, multi-step generation, or mixed open-ended and constrained outputs where confidence varies significantly step-to-step.
when_avoid: Avoid when latency is critical, models are uncalibrated, or you need strict reproducibility—entropy computation and adaptive sampling add jitter.

faq.q: How is entropy-based decoding different from temperature scheduling?
faq.a: Temperature scheduling adjusts temperature on a predefined schedule (e.g., linearly decrease over steps). Entropy-based decoding adjusts on the fly based on the actual distribution's uncertainty. Entropy adapts to what the model does; scheduling adapts to time.

faq.q: Does entropy-based decoding work with constrained decoding?
faq.a: Yes, but entropy is computed over the masked vocabulary (only valid tokens). If constraints eliminate most options, entropy drops artificially, and the benefit diminishes.

faq.q: What entropy threshold should I use?
faq.a: No universal threshold exists. Typical practice: log(vocabulary_size) / 2 as a starting point, then tune per model and task. High-entropy languages (e.g., code) often need higher thresholds than low-entropy domains (e.g., classification).

faq.q: Can entropy-based decoding reduce hallucinations?
faq.a: Indirectly—it tightens sampling when the model is confident, which can reduce unjustified variations. But entropy reflects uncertainty, not correctness; a confident wrong answer will still sample narrowly.

faq.q: Is entropy-based decoding the same as best-of-n sampling?
faq.a: No. Best-of-n generates multiple samples and ranks them; entropy-based decoding adapts a single forward pass. Entropy-based is cheaper but requires calibrated probabilities.

title: Frequency Penalty
tldr: Sampling parameter that reduces a token's probability based on how many times it has already appeared in the generated text.
plain_english: Frequency penalty discourages the model from repeating the same words over and over. Instead of a hard ban, it makes repeated words progressively less likely—the more times a word appears, the less chance it has of being selected next. It's like making a word slightly "tired" each time it gets used.
how_it_works: Before sampling, frequency penalty subtracts a penalty value (typically α × count) from each token's logit score, proportional to how many times that token has appeared in the output so far. A token that has appeared 3 times gets a larger penalty than one that appeared once. After this adjustment, the model samples from the penalized distribution. Range is typically 0 to 2: at 0, no penalty; at 2, heavily penalized tokens. Unlike presence penalty (flat subtraction per appearance), frequency penalty scales with count.
why_it_matters: Long-form outputs (articles, code, dialogue) naturally fall into repetitive patterns. Frequency penalty lets you reduce redundancy without manual post-processing or hard token blocking. It's a standard tuning knob in OpenAI's API and similar platforms, so understanding it directly improves generation quality for practical applications.
example: A chatbot generating customer support responses with a frequency penalty of 1.2 will repeat 'unfortunately' fewer times even though it's contextually relevant. On the first use, it has full probability; on the second, it's penalized by 1.2; on the third, by 2.4. This forces the model to find synonyms like 'regrettably' or 'sadly' while preserving semantic accuracy.
pitfalls: Setting frequency penalty too high (>1.5) forces unnatural word choices and can degrade coherence; test incrementally.
Frequency penalty counts across the entire output so far, making early tokens penalize later ones unpredictably in long sequences.
It penalizes function words (the, a, is) equally to content words, potentially harming grammar; consider presence penalty for different tradeoffs.
Frequency penalty is applied at inference time only—it does not affect what the model learned during training.
when_use: Use frequency penalty when generating long-form text (>500 tokens), creative content, or dialogue where natural repetition is unwanted and you want to avoid manual deduplication.
when_avoid: Avoid high frequency penalties when generating code, mathematical proofs, or outputs with necessary repeated terms; prefer presence penalty or no penalty for constrained domains.

faq.q: What's the difference between frequency penalty and presence penalty?
faq.a: Frequency penalty subtracts a value proportional to how many times a token has appeared (count × α). Presence penalty subtracts a fixed value after the token appears at all (flat -α). Frequency penalty scales with repetition; presence penalty does not.

faq.q: Will frequency penalty affect my token costs?
faq.a: No. Frequency penalty is applied during sampling and does not change the number of tokens generated or consumed. Cost is based on input and output token count, not penalty settings.

faq.q: What happens if I set frequency penalty to 0?
faq.a: At 0, no penalty is applied and the model samples from the original probability distribution. This is equivalent to disabling frequency penalty.

faq.q: Can I use frequency penalty with structured outputs?
faq.a: Frequency penalty is ignored when using constrained decoding modes (JSON mode, function calling) because token selection is forced by the schema, not by sampling.

faq.q: How do I know what frequency penalty value to use?
faq.a: Start at 0.5–1.0 for general long-form text and increment in 0.2 steps while sampling. Measure output quality and repetition; higher values reduce repetition but risk unnatural phrasing.

title: Greedy Decoding
tldr: Pick the highest-probability token at each step. Deterministic, fast, often repetitive.
plain_english: Greedy decoding is like always choosing the safest next word based on what the model knows right now. It's equivalent to setting temperature to zero—no randomness, no second-guessing. You feed in a prompt, and you get the exact same output every single time.
how_it_works: At each generation step, the model produces a probability distribution over all possible next tokens. Greedy decoding selects the token with the highest probability (argmax) and appends it to the sequence. This repeats until a stop token or max length is reached. Because there's no randomness, identical prompts always produce identical outputs. The process is fast because you skip sampling overhead and only compute one forward pass per token.
why_it_matters: Reproducibility matters for testing, compliance, and debugging. If you need eval scores or legal audit trails, greedy is the only decoding method that guarantees the same output every run. Most internal LLM benchmarks use temperature=0 for this reason—you can't fairly compare two models if your randomness adds noise to the scores.
example: An ML team evaluating JSON extraction from invoices runs the same 100 test documents through greedy decoding twice. Both runs produce byte-for-byte identical JSON. They switch to top-p sampling with temperature=0.7, and now run two produces different (and occasionally malformed) outputs. They revert to greedy for validation, accept that some creative tasks won't work, and use sampling only in staging.
pitfalls: Greedy often enters repetition loops ('I think I think I think') because it can't backtrack when a locally-best choice leads to a globally-bad sequence.
The model will confidently output a worse answer via greedy than via sampling, because it can't explore alternatives once committed to a token.
Greedy decoding ignores the full probability distribution—high second-place tokens are discarded, making the output brittle to small model changes.
when_use: Use greedy decoding when you need 100% reproducibility (eval harnesses, compliance logs, testing), or when the task has a single clearly-correct answer (structured data extraction, classification).
when_avoid: Avoid greedy for creative, open-ended, or conversational tasks—use temperature >0 or top-p sampling instead. Greedy often produces dull, repetitive, or stuck outputs in those domains.

faq.q: Is greedy decoding the same as temperature 0?
faq.a: Yes, in practice. Both disable randomness and always pick the argmax token. Some frameworks implement them identically; others may layer additional constraints, but the core behavior is the same.

faq.q: Why does greedy decoding repeat words?
faq.a: Once greedy picks a high-probability token (e.g., 'I'), that token becomes part of the context. The model then sees 'I' and assigns high probability to 'think' again. Greedy can't back out because it never samples alternatives.

faq.q: Can I use greedy decoding for creative writing?
faq.a: Technically yes, but results are usually poor. Greedy will produce stilted, repetitive, or clichéd text. Use top-p or temperature >0 for anything narrative or expressive.

faq.q: Does greedy decoding make the model faster?
faq.a: Slightly. You skip the sampling step and only compute one forward pass per token. But the difference is negligible—inference speed is usually bottlenecked by model size and hardware, not decoding method.

faq.q: How do I set greedy decoding in OpenAI or Anthropic APIs?
faq.a: Set temperature=0. OpenAI also accepts a seed parameter to lock randomness even further. Anthropic's APIs respect temperature=0 for greedy behavior.

title: Guidance (Microsoft)
tldr: Python library that mixes fixed template text with constrained LLM generation to guarantee structured output format.
plain_english: Guidance lets you write a generation template as a program—you write literal strings where output is fixed, and call generate() where the model fills in blanks. Think of it like a mail-merge template with type rules: you say 'name must match [a-z]+' and the model's constrained decoder respects that rule during token sampling. This avoids the parse-and-retry loop entirely.
how_it_works: You write a Python program mixing string literals and generate() calls. Each generate() call specifies a constraint—a regex, a type (int, bool), a choice list, or a JSON schema. Guidance uses constrained decoding to steer token sampling: at each step, only tokens that respect the constraint are allowed. Token healing skips regenerating fixed template sections when switching models. The library works with OpenAI, Anthropic, and local models via a unified API.
why_it_matters: Structured output without retry loops saves latency and tokens. Local model support (vs. JSON mode on OpenAI only) matters for teams running their own inference. Compared to prompt-then-parse, you get guaranteed format adherence and cleaner code—no post-hoc validation.
example: A customer service team building a support agent needs to extract ticket data in a fixed format. With Guidance, they write: `Dear customer, {{generate 'response' regex='[A-Za-z\s]{50,200}'}}. Ticket ID: {{generate 'ticket_id' regex='[0-9]{6}'}}`. The model fills in the gaps respecting regex constraints. Without Guidance, they'd generate freeform text, parse it, validate, and retry on mismatch.
pitfalls: Over-constraining with regexes can truncate valid outputs; test constraints against expected outputs first.
Token healing only works when switching between models with the same tokenizer; mismatched tokenizers lose the optimization.
Complex nested schemas with many generate() calls can reduce model reasoning quality by forcing too much structure too early.
Constrained decoding adds latency compared to greedy generation; measure overhead before committing to it in latency-critical paths.
when_use: Use Guidance when you need guaranteed output format (JSON, CSV, structured fields) and want to avoid parse-and-retry loops, especially with local or open-weight models.
when_avoid: Avoid when the output structure is genuinely open-ended (e.g., creative writing, exploratory reasoning), or when latency from constraint-checking matters more than format certainty.

faq.q: How does Guidance differ from just parsing the LLM output?
faq.a: Guidance enforces format constraints *during* token sampling, guaranteeing valid output on first pass. Parsing checks format *after* generation and requires retry logic on failure. Guidance eliminates retries and wasted tokens.

faq.q: Does Guidance work with local models?
faq.a: Yes. Guidance supports any model via constrained decoding at the token level. OpenAI's JSON mode is API-only; Guidance gives you the same guarantee on your own Llama, Mistral, or other local model.

faq.q: What's the performance cost of using Guidance?
faq.a: Constrained decoding adds latency per token (typically 5–20% slower than greedy sampling) because each token is checked against the constraint. Token healing reduces overhead when reusing fixed template sections across multiple calls.

faq.q: Can Guidance enforce complex nested JSON schemas?
faq.a: Yes, but deeply nested or very strict schemas can degrade model reasoning. Simpler constraints (regex, choice lists, basic types) work best; test complex schemas with your model first.

faq.q: Is Guidance the same as Outlines?
faq.a: Both do constrained decoding for structured output. Guidance is Microsoft's tool with an emphasis on mixing text and generation in a readable program; Outlines is more agnostic and focuses on schema-driven generation. Both solve similar problems.

title: Logit Bias
tldr: Add or subtract a constant from specific token logits before decoding to force, penalize, or ban outputs.
plain_english: Logit bias is a per-token adjustment you apply at inference time—before the model chooses its next word. Think of it like turning up the volume on tokens you want (yes, no) or muting ones you don't (proper names, profanity). You pass a dictionary mapping token IDs to bias values, typically between -100 and +100.
how_it_works: When the model generates a token, it computes logits (raw scores) for all possible tokens. You add your bias values to specific logits before softmax converts them to probabilities. A +100 bias makes a token almost certain; a -100 bias makes it nearly impossible. The operation happens once per forward pass, at the sampling stage, before temperature or other decoding parameters apply.
why_it_matters: Logit bias is the simplest constraint available for steering output without building a grammar parser or full structured-output system. Use it when you need hard guardrails on classification (force binary yes/no answers) or vocabulary (ban tokens) but don't need JSON schemas or complex rules.
example: An API endpoint classifying user feedback as positive, negative, or neutral. You find the token IDs for 'positive', 'negative', and 'neutral', then set logit_bias={'12345': 100, '67890': 100, '54321': 100}. The model can only output those three words, eliminating hallucinated labels and ensuring clean downstream parsing.
pitfalls: Finding token IDs requires decoding your tokenizer separately; IDs vary across model versions, so a fixed bias dict breaks on model updates.
Large negative biases (-100) don't guarantee a token never appears if the model assigns it extremely high logits naturally; they only reduce its probability.
Logit bias applies uniformly across all positions, so you can't steer the first token differently from the last without multiple API calls.
Combining logit bias with high temperature or top-p sampling can still let penalized tokens leak through if they compete with other high-probability tokens.
when_use: Use logit bias when you need to force or forbid specific tokens, enforce classification to a fixed set of labels, or ban tokens (profanity, domains) without building a full constrained-decoding pipeline.
when_avoid: Avoid logit bias when you need to enforce complex grammar, generate valid JSON, restrict multiple tokens across sequences, or when token IDs are unstable (frequent model updates).

faq.q: How do I find the token IDs for words I want to bias?
faq.a: Use your model's tokenizer.encode() method. For example, with OpenAI, decode the word into tokens, then map each token ID to your bias value. Test with a few words first—compound words may tokenize into multiple IDs.

faq.q: Can I use logit bias to force an exact output format like JSON?
faq.a: Not reliably. Logit bias works token-by-token but doesn't enforce structure across multiple positions. For JSON, use structured-output or constrained-decoding libraries like Outlines or Guidance instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between logit bias and temperature?
faq.a: Temperature scales all logits uniformly before softmax (higher = more random). Logit bias adds fixed offsets to specific tokens, letting you pin certain tokens up or down. You can use both together.

faq.q: Do token IDs change if I upgrade my model?
faq.a: Yes. Token IDs are tied to the model's tokenizer. Upgrading the model or tokenizer can shift IDs, breaking hardcoded bias dictionaries. Regenerate and test your logit_bias values after any model update.

faq.q: Can I combine logit bias with function calling?
faq.a: Yes. You can bias tokens to steer the model toward specific function names or parameters while still allowing the function-calling machinery to parse and validate the output.

title: Log Probability (Logprobs)
tldr: Natural logarithm of a token's probability; summed across sequences to measure confidence and detect hallucinations.
plain_english: Logprobs are the model's confidence score for each word it generates, expressed as a logarithm. Instead of saying "this word has a 0.05 probability," the model reports log(0.05), which is easier to work with mathematically. Sum them across a sentence and you get an overall confidence score—negative numbers mean lower confidence.
how_it_works: When a language model generates a token, it outputs logits (raw scores) that get converted to probabilities. The logarithm of that probability is the logprob. APIs like OpenAI's return top-K logprobs per token on request. To evaluate a full sequence, you sum the logprobs of each token; exponentiate that sum and you get perplexity. Low logprobs (more negative) indicate uncertain or unusual tokens; high logprobs (closer to zero) indicate confident predictions.
why_it_matters: Logprobs turn unstructured text into measurable signals you can act on. Use them to flag hallucinations (suspicious low-confidence passages), build confidence thresholds in production systems, or select hard examples for active learning. Without logprobs, you're evaluating models blind—guessing which outputs are trustworthy.
example: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system retrieves candidate documents and generates answers. By checking logprobs, the system detects when the model is inventing details with low confidence (logprob < −2 per token) and falls back to marking the answer as uncertain or requesting human review. A content moderation team uses logprobs to identify ambiguous classifications and route them to human annotators.
pitfalls: Logprobs are model-specific and not directly comparable across different model families—GPT-4 logprobs don't calibrate against Llama the same way.
Low logprobs don't always mean hallucination; they can signal valid uncertainty about genuinely ambiguous inputs.
Summing logprobs across sequences assumes independence, which breaks down when token choices are correlated.
Not all APIs expose logprobs by default; some require explicit requests or charge extra for them.
when_use: Use logprobs when you need to measure model confidence, detect anomalous outputs, implement confidence thresholds in production, or perform quantitative evaluation of generation quality.
when_avoid: Avoid treating logprobs as ground truth probabilities in high-stakes decisions; they reflect training data patterns, not true uncertainty. Skip logprob analysis if you're just prototyping and don't have access to API logprob outputs.

faq.q: What's the difference between logprobs and probabilities?
faq.a: Probabilities range from 0–1; logprobs are their natural logarithm and range from 0 to −∞. Logprobs compress small probabilities into readable numbers and make mathematical operations like summation more stable.

faq.q: How do I use logprobs to detect hallucinations?
faq.a: Set a confidence threshold (e.g., average logprob per token < −2) and flag outputs below it. Low logprobs often correlate with invented facts, but pair this signal with other checks—uncertainty alone isn't proof of hallucination.

faq.q: Can I compare logprobs across different models?
faq.a: No. Logprobs are model-specific because different models have different vocabularies, training objectives, and calibration. A logprob of −1.5 in GPT-4 doesn't mean the same thing as −1.5 in Llama.

faq.q: Do all LLM APIs return logprobs?
faq.a: No. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Groq expose logprobs, but some endpoints require you to request them explicitly or may charge a small overhead. Check your API documentation.

faq.q: What's the relationship between logprobs and perplexity?
faq.a: Perplexity is exp(−mean logprob per token) over a full sequence. It measures how surprised the model is by the data; lower perplexity means higher confidence.

title: Min-P Sampling
tldr: Sampling method that adapts token cutoff relative to the top token's probability, keeping only candidates above a minimum threshold.
plain_english: Min-P fixes a quirk in top-p sampling: when a model is very confident (say, 90% sure about one token), top-p can still pull in random low-probability alternatives. Min-P instead sets a floor relative to the best choice—if the top token scores 0.9 and you set min-p to 0.05, only tokens scoring 0.045 or higher get included. This scales the cutoff with the model's confidence.
how_it_works: You set a min-p value (typically 0.05–0.1). The algorithm finds the highest-probability token, multiplies its score by min-p to get a threshold, then includes all tokens above that threshold in the sampling pool. When the top token is 0.9, threshold = 0.045. When top is 0.1 (uncertain), threshold = 0.005, letting more tokens through. Tokens are then sampled using multinomial distribution over the kept set.
why_it_matters: Min-P produces more coherent long-form text than top-p at similar diversity levels because it respects model confidence. When the model is sure, it stays on topic; when uncertain, it explores. This reduces the "random tangent" problem common in top-p generation without requiring manual temperature or top-p tuning per context.
example: A creative writing system using top-p=0.9 generates stilted dialogue because it occasionally samples unlikely word choices even when the model strongly prefers specific tokens. Switching to min-p=0.05 yields more natural prose: the model's confident passages stay coherent, but during open-ended scenes it still explores alternatives. No other hyperparameter changes needed.
pitfalls: Min-p=0 includes all tokens (no filtering); values above 0.2 become very restrictive—test empirically.
Combining min-p with temperature scaling can over-constrain low-confidence outputs; use one or the other.
Min-p ignores absolute probabilities, so a 0.01 top token with min-p=0.05 sets floor at 0.0005, including almost all tokens.
Not all frameworks implement min-p yet; verify your inference server supports it.
when_use: Use min-p when you need stable, topic-coherent generation (essays, code, dialogue) and you want sampling diversity without tuning temperature per prompt.
when_avoid: Avoid min-p if you need greedy or deterministic output, or if your inference stack doesn't support it—use temperature or top-p instead.

faq.q: How is min-p different from top-p?
faq.a: Top-p filters by cumulative probability (e.g., include tokens until 90% mass is covered). Min-p filters by absolute probability relative to the best token. Min-p adapts cutoff dynamically; top-p is fixed regardless of model confidence.

faq.q: What min-p value should I use?
faq.a: Start with 0.05–0.1. Lower values (0.02) allow more exploration; higher values (0.15+) are more conservative. Test on your use case; there's no universal setting.

faq.q: Can I use min-p and temperature together?
faq.a: Yes, but they interact: temperature flattens the probability distribution, which can make min-p's threshold very permissive. Use one or the other to avoid over-tuning.

faq.q: Does min-p work better for creative or technical writing?
faq.a: It works well for both. For technical writing (code, docs), it keeps output on-topic. For creative work, it lets the model explore when uncertain but stabilizes confident passages.

faq.q: Which inference servers support min-p?
faq.a: vLLM, LM Studio, Ollama, and some others support it. Check your framework's sampling API; if not, you may need to implement it as a post-processing filter.

title: Mirostat
tldr: Sampling method that adjusts token cutoff at each step to maintain target output perplexity.
plain_english: Mirostat treats text generation like a control problem: you set a target "surprise level" (perplexity), and the algorithm automatically tightens or loosens which tokens are allowed at each step to stay on target. Think of it like cruise control for generation quality—instead of a fixed speed limit, you're aiming for consistent difficulty throughout a long output.
how_it_works: You set a target perplexity τ (typically 5–10). At each decoding step, Mirostat computes the entropy of the token distribution and adjusts a scaling parameter to bring sampled tokens closer to that target. Mirostat v2 simplifies this with a single learning rate that updates after each token. The result: if generation starts drifting toward repetitive (low perplexity) or chaotic (high perplexity) text, the algorithm corrects course automatically. This works across any context length without retuning.
why_it_matters: Long-form generation (stories, code, summaries) often degrades with fixed samplers: early tokens stay coherent, then quality collapses into loops or nonsense. Mirostat keeps output quality consistent by maintaining entropy discipline, eliminating manual temperature tweaking mid-generation and reducing user frustration with 10k-token outputs that start strong but decay.
example: A fiction writer using llama.cpp generates a 5,000-token short story. With top-p sampling alone, the narrative stays tight for 1,000 tokens, then repeats phrases. With Mirostat τ=6, the same model maintains balanced pacing throughout—descriptions stay vivid without digressing, and dialogue stays natural without repetition. No manual temperature adjustment needed.
pitfalls: Setting τ too low (< 3) locks you into safe, repetitive text; too high (> 10) erodes coherence. Test on your actual model and use case.
Mirostat adds ~5–10% compute overhead per token due to entropy calculation and parameter updates—noticeable on edge devices.
Combining Mirostat with other dynamic samplers (like frequency penalty or repetition penalty) can cause conflicting corrections; use one at a time.
Mirostat assumes the model's logit distribution is meaningful; on undertrained or fine-tuned models with skewed output, it may overcorrect.
when_use: Use Mirostat for long-form generation (>2,000 tokens) where output quality must stay consistent, especially in creative or technical domains where manual temperature tuning isn't feasible.
when_avoid: Avoid Mirostat for short single-turn outputs (<500 tokens) where fixed temperature works fine, or when you need frame-by-frame control over randomness (use top-p or temperature instead).

faq.q: What's the difference between Mirostat and top-p sampling?
faq.a: Top-p uses a fixed cutoff for token probability mass; Mirostat dynamically adjusts that cutoff per step to hit a perplexity target. Top-p is static, Mirostat is adaptive.

faq.q: What perplexity value should I use?
faq.a: Start with τ = 5–8 for creative writing, 3–5 for code, 7–10 for casual chat. Tune by generating a sample and checking if output feels repetitive (lower τ) or chaotic (higher τ).

faq.q: Does Mirostat work with all models?
faq.a: Yes, Mirostat works with any model producing logits. It's model-agnostic but may need τ tuning per architecture (LLaMA vs. Mistral behave slightly differently).

faq.q: Is Mirostat slower than top-p?
faq.a: Yes, slightly—Mirostat recalculates entropy and updates parameters at every token, adding ~5–10% latency. For real-time chat, consider top-p unless consistency is critical.

faq.q: Can I use Mirostat with quantized models?
faq.a: Yes. Quantization doesn't change the logits Mirostat reads, so it works fine with GGUF, AWQ, or other quantized formats in llama.cpp or vLLM.

title: Multinomial Sampling
tldr: Draw the next token by randomly sampling from the full vocabulary distribution weighted by model probabilities.
plain_english: Multinomial sampling is the baseline way to add randomness to text generation. Instead of always picking the most likely next word, you roll a weighted die where each vocabulary word's face size matches its predicted probability. Temperature controls how fair or biased the die is—lower temperature makes high-probability words almost certain, higher temperature makes rare words more competitive.
how_it_works: First, the model outputs logits (raw scores) for each token in its vocabulary. Apply softmax with your chosen temperature to convert logits into probabilities that sum to 1. Then sample one token from that distribution—high-probability tokens are more likely to be selected, but any token can win. Repeat for the next position. With temperature=1 you use raw softmax probabilities; temperature→0 approaches greedy decoding; temperature>1 flattens the distribution and increases randomness.
why_it_matters: Multinomial sampling is the reference point every other sampling method builds on or modifies. Understanding the baseline—and why it produces high-variance low-quality outputs when vocabulary is large—explains why practitioners use top-p, top-k, or min-p to truncate the tail before sampling. It's also the only method that samples the full probability space without approximation.
example: A chatbot generates responses using multinomial sampling with temperature=0.7. The model predicts token probabilities: [hello: 0.4, hi: 0.3, hey: 0.2, blarg: 0.1]. On the first roll, 'hello' wins 40% of the time, 'hi' 30%, 'hey' 20%, and the nonsense word 'blarg' 10%. Over 100 generations, 'blarg' will appear roughly 10 times—introducing noise that degrades output quality. This motivates switching to top-k or top-p sampling instead.
pitfalls: Long-tail noise: low-probability tokens still get sampled, degrading coherence. Most production systems add top-k/top-p truncation before multinomial sampling.
Temperature mistuning: temperature=0 breaks backprop if you need gradients; temperature>2 often produces gibberish. Standard range is 0.5–1.5.
Inefficiency at scale: sampling the full vocabulary is slower than top-k alternatives when vocabulary is 50k+ tokens.
Reproducibility: multinomial sampling is stochastic, so identical inputs produce different outputs unless you seed the random number generator.
when_use: Use multinomial sampling when you need a reference baseline for comparison, or when vocabulary is small (<5k tokens) and you want true probability coverage without truncation.
when_avoid: Avoid in production generation without top-k/top-p filtering, when you need deterministic output, or when latency is critical at large vocabulary size.

faq.q: How is multinomial sampling different from greedy decoding?
faq.a: Greedy decoding always picks the single highest-probability token. Multinomial sampling rolls a weighted die using the full probability distribution, so any token can be chosen—higher-probability tokens just win more often. Multinomial adds randomness; greedy is deterministic.

faq.q: What does temperature do in multinomial sampling?
faq.a: Temperature rescales the probability distribution before sampling. Lower temperature (e.g., 0.3) makes high-probability tokens even more dominant; higher temperature (e.g., 1.5) flattens the distribution and gives rare tokens a better chance to be sampled.

faq.q: Why do people use top-p instead of pure multinomial sampling?
faq.a: Pure multinomial sampling includes the long tail of very-low-probability tokens, which adds noise and incoherence. Top-p truncates the distribution to only the most-probable tokens that sum to a threshold (e.g., 90%), then samples from that smaller set. This keeps diversity while eliminating garbage tokens.

faq.q: Can multinomial sampling be used with gradient-based training?
faq.a: Direct sampling is non-differentiable. For training, use Gumbel-softmax or straight-through estimators as alternatives. At inference (generation), multinomial sampling is fine.

faq.q: Is multinomial sampling faster or slower than beam search?
faq.a: Multinomial is faster: one token per forward pass, single sample. Beam search keeps K hypotheses in parallel, requiring K forward passes per step, so it's slower but explores more output space.

title: Outlines (Structured LLM)
tldr: Open-source library that enforces exact output formats (JSON, regex, grammar) by masking invalid tokens during generation.
plain_english: Outlines compiles a schema or grammar into a set of rules that the model must follow at each generation step. Instead of letting the model pick any token, Outlines calculates which tokens are valid at that moment—like autocomplete that only offers words that fit your grammar. This guarantees the output will always match your schema, not just sometimes.
how_it_works: You define a schema (Pydantic model, JSON schema, regex, or Lark grammar) and pass it to Outlines. Outlines converts the schema into a finite automaton over your tokenizer's vocabulary, precomputing valid next tokens for each state. During generation, at each step Outlines masks invalid tokens (sets their logits to -∞), forcing the model to choose only from legal continuations. This runs in O(1) per token with no re-parsing, and integrates with Transformers, vLLM, and llama.cpp.
why_it_matters: Constrained decoding without Outlines requires either expensive re-ranking, slower token-by-token validation, or accepting invalid outputs and post-processing (which fails silently). Outlines eliminates that tradeoff: you get guaranteed valid JSON or regex matches at production speed. It's the default choice when you need structured outputs at scale without latency penalties.
example: A fintech API generates loan application decisions in strict JSON. Without Outlines, you'd reject 15–20% of outputs for malformed fields, adding retry latency and complexity. With Outlines and a Pydantic schema, every token generation respects the schema—no rejections, no post-processing, output is always valid structured data on first attempt.
pitfalls: Automaton compilation is one-time cost per schema, but complex grammars (deep nesting, many alternatives) can create large state machines; test schema complexity before production.
Token masking only works if your tokenizer vocabulary aligns with the schema; rare characters or multi-token sequences may fail silently or reduce throughput.
Outlines constrains token choice, which can reduce model quality or diversity if the schema is overly restrictive; always compare constrained vs. unconstrained outputs on your use case.
Integrating Outlines with custom model servers or quantized models (GPTQ, AWQ) requires version matching; incompatible versions fall back to slower validation modes.
when_use: Use Outlines when you need guaranteed valid output format (JSON, SQL, regex) at scale, can define your schema upfront, and prioritize latency and correctness over sampling flexibility.
when_avoid: Avoid Outlines if your output format is undefined, highly variable, or requires creative diversity; also avoid if you're using a model server without Outlines integration (adds latency).

faq.q: What's the difference between Outlines and Guidance?
faq.a: Guidance uses context-free grammars with more flexible token-level control; Outlines precompiles automata for speed. Outlines is faster for rigid schemas; Guidance is more flexible for complex branching logic.

faq.q: Does Outlines guarantee the output is valid JSON?
faq.a: Yes, if you provide a valid JSON schema. Outlines masks all tokens that would produce invalid JSON, so generation can only produce valid output.

faq.q: How much does Outlines slow down generation?
faq.a: Minimal—token masking is O(1) per step and compiled upfront. Real-world overhead is <5% on most models; in some cases it's faster than beam search because fewer tokens are explored.

faq.q: Can I use Outlines with quantized models like GPTQ?
faq.a: Yes, if your inference engine (vLLM, llama.cpp) supports both. Verify version compatibility; older versions may require slower fallback validation.

faq.q: What happens if my schema is too complex?
faq.a: Automaton compilation time increases and memory usage grows, but generation itself stays fast. Test large schemas offline first; if compilation is slow, simplify or split the schema.

acceptedAnswer.text: Minimal—token masking is O(1) per step and compiled upfront. Real-world overhead is <5% on most models; in some cases it's faster than beam search because fewer tokens are explored.

mainEntity.name: Can I use Outlines with quantized models like GPTQ?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if your inference engine (vLLM, llama.cpp) supports both. Verify version compatibility; older versions may require slower fallback validation.

mainEntity.name: What happens if my schema is too complex?

acceptedAnswer.text: Automaton compilation time increases and memory usage grows, but generation itself stays fast. Test large schemas offline first; if compilation is slow, simplify or split the schema.

title: Perplexity
tldr: Exponentiated average negative log-likelihood of a sequence; lower means the model finds it more natural.
plain_english: Perplexity measures how surprised a language model is by a sequence of text. If a model has perplexity 10 on a test set, it's roughly equally uncertain between 10 equally likely next tokens on average. It's a single number that tells you how well the model predicts text it hasn't seen before—lower is always better.
how_it_works: For each token in a sequence, the model outputs a probability based on preceding context. Perplexity = exp(−(1/N) Σ log p(token_i | context)), where N is sequence length and p is the model's predicted probability. You compute log-probabilities across held-out text, average them, negate, exponentiate. The formula converts likelihood into an intuitive scale: PPL of 10 means the model's best guess is diluted across ~10 equally plausible tokens. It only measures prediction quality on the language itself, not task performance or alignment.
why_it_matters: Perplexity is the standard intrinsic metric for evaluating base language models. Every pre-trained LLM is ranked on held-out perplexity during development. It's faster to compute than task-specific metrics and independent of downstream applications, making it the first signal of model quality. Teams use it to compare model architectures, tokenizers, and training recipes without waiting for task evaluations.
example: A team at Anthropic trains two versions of a 7B model: one with standard tokenization and one with a refined vocabulary. On held-out Wikipedia text, model A achieves PPL 15.2, model B achieves PPL 16.8. They pick model A for further instruction-tuning because the lower perplexity suggests better language understanding. However, when both hit production, their chat quality (measured by user ratings) is nearly identical—revealing that perplexity alone didn't predict task performance.
pitfalls: Perplexity is not directly comparable across different tokenizers—smaller vocabularies inflate apparent perplexity on identical text because each token carries more information.
Low perplexity on clean text (e.g. Wikipedia) does not guarantee good performance on noisy, out-of-distribution inputs or specialized domains.
Perplexity says nothing about alignment, instruction-following, hallucination rates, or task-specific accuracy; a low-perplexity model can still fail at reasoning or factuality.
Using perplexity to compare models trained on different data distributions or with different batch sizes can be misleading without controlling for confounds.
when_use: Use perplexity to compare language models during pre-training, validate tokenizer changes, or detect distribution shift on held-out test sets.
when_avoid: Avoid perplexity as your only evaluation metric for fine-tuned models, multi-task systems, or any application where task accuracy matters more than raw prediction likelihood.

faq.q: What is a good perplexity score for a language model?
faq.a: Context-dependent. State-of-the-art 7B models on Wikipedia achieve 10–15 PPL; on code, 15–25 PPL is typical. Smaller models or harder domains see higher scores. Always compare within the same dataset and tokenizer.

faq.q: Can I compare perplexity across different models if they use different tokenizers?
faq.a: No—a model with a smaller vocabulary will appear more perplexed on the same text because it expresses the same meaning across more tokens. Re-tokenize to the same vocabulary or use a normalized comparison.

faq.q: Does low perplexity mean the model will be good at my task?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Perplexity measures language prediction quality, not reasoning, alignment, or task-specific accuracy. A low-perplexity model can still hallucinate, refuse correctly, or fail at reasoning.

faq.q: Why do you use exponentiation in the perplexity formula?
faq.a: Exponentiation converts average negative log-likelihood into an intuitive branching factor: PPL of 100 means the model is roughly equally uncertain between 100 tokens, making the number easier to reason about than raw loss values.

faq.q: How is perplexity different from cross-entropy loss?
faq.a: Cross-entropy is the average negative log-likelihood (the exponent in PPL). Perplexity is the exponentiated form. Both measure the same underlying prediction quality; PPL is just more interpretable.
