title: Presence Penalty
tldr: Reduces token probability if it appeared anywhere before, encouraging topic diversity in generation.
plain_english: Presence penalty is a flat penalty applied to any token the model has already generated. Unlike frequency penalty, which scales with repetition count, presence penalty gives the same penalty regardless of how many times a token appeared. Higher values push the model toward new ideas; lower or negative values let it stay on-topic.
how_it_works: During decoding, the model's logit (raw score before softmax) for each previously-seen token is reduced by a fixed amount (typically 0.1–1.0). This happens once per token, independent of how many times it appeared. The penalty is applied before softmax probability calculation, making less-likely tokens even less likely. At inference time, you set the penalty value and it applies uniformly across the entire generation. Negative values reverse the effect, penalizing unseen tokens instead.
why_it_matters: In brainstorming, summarization, and creative writing tasks, models tend to loop through the same phrases. Presence penalty breaks that loop by making any already-used word harder to pick again, forcing lexical and conceptual variety. This matters because repetition kills user engagement and makes outputs feel stale, even if factually correct.
example: An OpenAI API user generating product feature ideas sets presence_penalty=0.6. When the model suggests 'innovation' in idea #1, that word's logit drops by 0.6 in all future ideas, making it much less likely to reappear. Over 10 ideas, the user gets 'innovation,' 'breakthrough,' 'advancement,' 'disruption'—varied phrasings instead of 'innovation' repeated five times.
pitfalls: Presence penalty only checks if a token appeared at all, not how relevant it is; removing common words like 'the' entirely breaks coherence.
Applying high presence penalty (>1.0) to long generations often produces grammatical errors or off-topic sentences as the model exhausts natural vocabulary.
Presence and frequency penalty stack; using both together can over-suppress common tokens and degrade output quality.
Negative presence penalty (encouraging repetition) rarely works as intended; use frequency penalty with negative value instead.
when_use: Use presence penalty for brainstorming, creative writing, diverse candidate generation, or any task where output monotony signals poor quality.
when_avoid: Avoid presence penalty for factual Q&A, technical documentation, or translation where repeating key terms is essential; use frequency penalty instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between presence penalty and frequency penalty?
faq.a: Presence penalty subtracts a fixed amount once per token, regardless of count. Frequency penalty scales the penalty by how many times the token appeared. Use presence for diversity; use frequency to discourage repeated words proportionally.

faq.q: Does presence penalty slow down inference?
faq.a: No. It's a single lookup and subtraction per token during logit calculation—negligible overhead at decode time.

faq.q: Can I use presence penalty with instruct models?
faq.a: Yes. Presence penalty works on any model during generation. However, instruct models already favor variety through training, so you may need lower values (0.2–0.4) to avoid degrading coherence.

faq.q: What happens if I set presence penalty too high?
faq.a: The model runs out of common tokens and begins generating rare, incoherent, or off-topic words to avoid penalties. Start with 0.5–0.6 and adjust downward if quality drops.

faq.q: Does presence penalty work for multi-turn conversations?
faq.a: Yes, but it penalizes tokens across the entire conversation history. This can prevent the model from reusing proper nouns or relevant context; consider disabling it for dialogue or using a smaller value.

title: Reasoning Token / Thinking Budget
tldr: A cap on internal reasoning tokens a model can spend before returning its answer, trading compute cost for solution quality.
plain_english: Reasoning models like o1 and Claude with extended thinking can spend minutes thinking through a problem before answering. A thinking budget is a limit you set—like "use up to 10,000 tokens to reason, then answer." High budgets let the model work through hard problems; low budgets cut costs when the question is straightforward.
how_it_works: You pass a parameter (reasoning_effort: low/medium/high, or max_reasoning_tokens: N) when calling a reasoning model. The model allocates that token budget to internal reasoning—working through logic, checking steps, exploring alternatives—before emitting the final answer. Once the budget is spent, it stops reasoning and outputs. The reasoning itself is hidden; you only see the final response and optional reasoning trace. This creates a direct trade-off: more tokens = slower, more expensive, often better answers; fewer tokens = faster, cheaper, riskier.
why_it_matters: Reasoning models can cost 10–50x more per query than standard LLMs if left uncapped. Without a budget, a single math problem or code-review request can burn through dollars. Setting the right budget per task—strict limits on simple queries, generous budgets on proofs or system design—is essential for cost control in production reasoning deployments.
example: A customer support team uses Claude with extended thinking to debug user issues. Simple password-reset requests get reasoning_effort: low (budget ~1,000 tokens); complex account-billing disputes get medium (5,000 tokens); production outage root-cause analysis gets high (20,000+ tokens). Over a month, tight budgeting on low-value tickets saves 60% in reasoning costs while maintaining solution quality where it matters.
pitfalls: Setting the same budget for all queries wastes money on simple questions and starves complex ones of compute.
Assuming 'high' effort always produces better answers—diminishing returns kick in; 15,000 tokens may not beat 5,000 on a task where the model finds the answer quickly.
Ignoring reasoning latency: high budgets can add 30–60 seconds per request, breaking real-time SLA targets.
Not measuring the reasoning output; many models hide the internal trace, so you can't audit whether the budget was actually used effectively.
when_use: Use reasoning budgets when deploying o1, o3, Claude extended thinking, or DeepSeek-R1—any model with explicit test-time compute scaling—especially in cost-sensitive or latency-aware production systems.
when_avoid: Avoid reasoning budgets when using standard chat models (GPT-4, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Mistral) that don't support internal reasoning phases, or when latency is non-negotiable and reasoning adds unacceptable delay.

faq.q: What's the difference between reasoning budget and context window?
faq.a: Context window is the total input+output tokens your model can handle. Reasoning budget is a subset—tokens spent on hidden internal thinking before the final answer. A 128K context window model might have a 10K reasoning budget.

faq.q: Can I set a reasoning budget as an explicit token count or only as a preset level?
faq.a: It depends on the model. OpenAI's o1 accepts only reasoning_effort (low/medium/high); some APIs (e.g., certain Claude versions) accept max_thinking_tokens as a number. Check your provider's API docs.

faq.q: Does a higher reasoning budget guarantee a better answer?
faq.a: No. Most answers stabilize after a certain threshold. Beyond that, extra tokens are wasted. Profile your use case empirically—often medium budget is sufficient, and high is only needed for competition-grade math or system design.

faq.q: How much does reasoning cost compared to standard inference?
faq.a: Reasoning tokens typically cost 3–10x more than standard output tokens. A 10K reasoning + 500 output token request may cost $0.50–$2.00 depending on the model and provider.

faq.q: Can I see what the model was thinking during the reasoning phase?
faq.a: Sometimes. OpenAI's o1 and Claude's extended thinking can return a thinking trace, but it's not always enabled by default. Check your API response structure; some providers omit it to reduce latency.

title: Repetition Penalty
tldr: Reduces the probability of tokens already in the context, stopping models from looping on the same words.
plain_english: Repetition penalty makes previously-generated words less likely to appear again. Think of it like a deck of cards where cards you've already drawn get removed before your next draw. Too weak, and the model keeps saying "the the the"; too strong, and it avoids natural repetition, making sentences sound robotic.
how_it_works: Before softmax, the logits of tokens that appeared in the generated sequence are divided by a penalty factor (typically 1.1–1.3). Lower logits mean lower selection probability. You control the strength: 1.0 = no penalty, higher values = stronger penalty. Most inference engines apply this by default during decoding. Unlike n-gram blocking (which forbids exact sequences), repetition penalty uses a continuous reduction.
why_it_matters: Loop-free generation is table-stakes for production inference. Without it, models degrade into repetitive gibberish on long outputs. It's cheap to apply and every inference stack exposes the parameter, making it the first knob to turn when you see repeated phrases.
example: A chatbot generating customer support responses might set repetition-penalty to 1.2. Without it, a response about refunds might read: "Your refund refund refund will process in 5–7 days." With the penalty, the same input produces: "Your refund will process in 5–7 days." The word 'refund' still appears naturally once, but doesn't spiral.
pitfalls: Penalty too high (>1.5) forces dodging of common words like 'the', 'and', producing stilted, unnatural text.
Penalty applied globally ignores context — proper names and domain terms may need exemptions.
Confusing with frequency-penalty (which penalizes by count) or presence-penalty (which penalizes once per token); repetition-penalty divides logits directly.
No effect on already-memorized patterns in weights; it only constrains decoding-time choices.
when_use: Use repetition-penalty when generating longer outputs (>100 tokens), open-ended text, or any task where uncontrolled loops are common.
when_avoid: Avoid or lower it heavily when generating code, structured data, or names where exact repetition is semantically required.

faq.q: What's the difference between repetition penalty and frequency penalty?
faq.a: Repetition penalty divides logits uniformly for all seen tokens. Frequency penalty subtracts a score scaled by how many times a token appeared. Frequency gives diminishing returns; repetition is a hard divisor.

faq.q: How do I know if my repetition penalty is too high?
faq.a: Read the output. If it avoids common words or sounds unnatural (e.g., 'The cat sat on the furniture' instead of 'The cat sat on the rug'), reduce the penalty from 1.3 to 1.1.

faq.q: Does repetition penalty prevent hallucinations?
faq.a: No. It only discourages token repetition. Hallucinations come from training or context, not loop behavior. Use separate techniques like RAG or guardrails for that.

faq.q: Can I apply repetition penalty to specific tokens only?
faq.a: Most inference engines apply it globally. Some frameworks (e.g., vLLM, Outlines) allow custom sampling logic to exempt or weight certain tokens differently.

faq.q: What's a good starting value for repetition penalty?
faq.a: 1.1–1.2 works for most tasks. Start there and adjust down if output feels stilted, up if you still see loops.

title: Stop Sequences
tldr: Strings that halt model output when generated, used to delimit structured responses or prevent over-generation.
plain_english: Stop sequences are instructions you give to a language model: "when you generate this exact text, stop immediately." Think of them like a red light in the middle of a sentence. Common examples are blank lines (to stop at paragraph breaks), closing tags (to end structured blocks), or conversation markers (to stop before the next speaker).
how_it_works: During decoding, the inference engine scans each newly generated token and checks it against your stop sequence list. The moment a stop sequence matches, generation halts and that sequence is not included in the output. You can specify multiple stop sequences at once—the model stops at whichever appears first. Most APIs apply stop sequences before max_tokens limits, so they work as a primary termination mechanism. Use them alongside max_tokens for defense-in-depth: stop sequences handle the happy path, max_tokens catch runaway cases.
why_it_matters: Stop sequences are the simplest constraint on generation that don't require expensive techniques like constrained decoding. They let you bound model output and ensure structured responses without custom grammars or token masking, making them practical for APIs, chat turns, and templated outputs.
example: A chat application uses stop sequences ["User:", "Assistant:"] to prevent the model from writing multiple turns on its own. When the model generates "User:", the API stops immediately, returning only the assistant's response. This is cheaper and faster than full constrained decoding, and Anthropic, OpenAI, and other providers use this as the default termination strategy for chat interfaces.
pitfalls: Stop sequences are substring matches, not token boundaries—a sequence spanning token boundaries may not trigger reliably across all tokenizers.
Multiple stop sequences can conflict; if one is a prefix of another, the shorter one always triggers first, potentially cutting off valid output.

Whitespace matters exactly: "

" (two newlines) is different from " 

" (space + two newlines), and trailing spaces in your sequence are often invisible bugs.
Stop sequences don't prevent hallucination or steer content—they only truncate after generation, so the model can still produce unsafe or incorrect text before the sequence appears.
when_use: Use stop sequences when you need to end generation at a predictable marker (dialog turns, closing tags, section breaks) and the marker reliably appears in the model's output.
when_avoid: Avoid stop sequences if the marker is rare or unpredictable in output, or if you need semantic guarantees (e.g., ensuring JSON validity)—use constrained decoding or guided generation instead.

faq.q: What's the difference between stop sequences and max_tokens?
faq.a: Stop sequences halt generation when specific text appears; max_tokens halts after a fixed count. Use stop sequences for semantic boundaries (like dialog turns) and max_tokens as a safety cap. Both can be active at once.

faq.q: Do stop sequences work the same way in all APIs?
faq.a: Most APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Hugging Face) implement stop sequences identically—substring matching during streaming—but some older or niche services may differ. Check documentation for exact behavior.

faq.q: Can I use stop sequences with constrained decoding?
faq.a: Yes. Stop sequences are often faster and simpler for coarse truncation, while constrained decoding ensures strict format compliance. Layer them: use constrained decoding for JSON schema, stop sequences for dialog boundaries.

faq.q: What happens if the stop sequence never appears?
faq.a: Generation continues until max_tokens is reached. If you need guaranteed termination, always pair stop sequences with max_tokens.

faq.q: Are stop sequences case-sensitive?
faq.a: Yes. "User:" and "user:" are different sequences. Exact string matching applies.

title: Temperature
tldr: A parameter scaling how much randomness the model applies when picking the next token.
plain_english: Temperature controls whether your model plays it safe or takes risks. At 0, it always picks the most likely next word (like reading a predictable script). At 1.0, it samples words proportionally to their probabilities (normal conversation). Higher values make it more chaotic and unpredictable.
how_it_works: Temperature rescales the probability distribution over possible next tokens before sampling. At temperature 0, the model applies greedy decoding—it selects the single highest-probability token every time, producing deterministic output. At temperature 1.0, probabilities stay unchanged and the model samples stochastically. At temperatures above 1.0, the distribution flattens, making low-probability tokens more likely to be chosen. The math: divide logits by T before softmax. Higher T = flatter, more uniform distribution; lower T = sharper, more concentrated distribution.
why_it_matters: Temperature is one of the most direct controls over output quality and consistency. Set it too low and your model produces robotic repetition; set it too high and you get hallucinations, incoherence, and unreliable behavior. For deterministic tasks (code, math), you want low temperature. For creative or open-ended tasks, higher temperature gives variety without breaking coherence.
example: A customer-support chatbot would use temperature 0.3–0.5 to answer account questions reliably and identically each time. A creative writing assistant for brainstorming might use 0.8–1.0 to generate diverse plot ideas. An API for summarization would lock temperature at 0 to ensure consistency across identical requests.
pitfalls: Setting temperature too high (>1.5) causes the model to ignore probabilities and generate random nonsense; you lose the model's learned knowledge.
Confusing temperature with top-p or top-k; temperature reshapes the entire distribution before any truncation happens.
Using high temperature for code generation or factual tasks; the model will invent plausible-sounding but wrong answers.
Assuming temperature 0 is always safer; it can produce repetitive, evasive, or stilted responses in conversational contexts.
when_use: Use temperature >0.7 for creative writing, brainstorming, and open-ended generation; use 0.3–0.5 for classification, summarization, and factual retrieval; use 0 only when determinism is critical (e.g., reproducible evaluations).
when_avoid: Avoid high temperature (>1.0) for code, math, or factual Q&A; avoid temperature 0 if you need natural, varied conversational tone; avoid changing temperature mid-conversation without resetting context.

faq.q: What is the difference between temperature 0 and temperature 1?
faq.a: Temperature 0 always picks the highest-probability token (deterministic, repetitive). Temperature 1.0 samples proportionally to the original probabilities (stochastic, natural). Temperature 0 is useful for reproducibility; temperature 1.0 gives more natural variation.

faq.q: What temperature should I use for code generation?
faq.a: Use temperature 0–0.3. Code correctness depends on the model following learned patterns closely. High temperature introduces hallucinated syntax and logic errors.

faq.q: Can I use temperature with top-p sampling?
faq.a: Yes. Temperature reshapes the probability distribution first, then top-p truncates it. Using both together gives you control over both diversity and extreme-tail probability cutoff.

faq.q: Why does my model produce the same response every time?
faq.a: You likely have temperature set to 0 or very close to it. Increase temperature to 0.7–1.0 to get varied responses; even then, the prompt and model weights matter more than temperature alone.

faq.q: Is higher temperature always better for creativity?
faq.a: No. Temperature >1.5 often produces incoherent gibberish rather than creative ideas. Aim for 0.7–1.0; creativity comes from the model's training, not from chaos.

mainEntity.name: Is higher temperature always better for creativity?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Temperature >1.5 often produces incoherent gibberish rather than creative ideas. Aim for 0.7–1.0; creativity comes from the model's training, not from chaos.

title: Top-k Sampling
tldr: Sampling strategy that restricts choices to the K highest-probability tokens, ignoring the rest.
plain_english: When a language model generates text, it assigns a probability score to every word it knows. Top-k sampling says: "Only consider the top K most likely options, throw away the rest, then randomly pick one." It's like narrowing a restaurant menu to your 40 favorite dishes instead of reading all 500 options.
how_it_works: At each generation step, the model scores all tokens in its vocabulary. The decoder selects the K tokens with the highest scores, renormalizes their probabilities to sum to 1.0, then samples one token from that restricted distribution. Common K values range from 40 to 100. A small K (e.g., 5) produces repetitive, formulaic output; a large K (e.g., 500) approaches unrestricted sampling and reintroduces rare implausible tokens. Top-k is often combined with top-p (nucleus sampling) in practice: top-k acts as a hard ceiling, top-p adds adaptive cutoff based on cumulative probability.
why_it_matters: Top-k is a foundational decoding method exposed by nearly all LLM inference stacks. Understanding it unlocks why temperature alone doesn't control output quality, how beam search and other strategies differ, and how to avoid pathological behaviors like hallucinating nonsense tokens during low-confidence moments.
example: You're deploying a chatbot on vLLM with `SamplingParams(top_k=50, top_p=0.9, temperature=0.7)`. At each token, the model considers its full 32k-token vocabulary, but the decoder only allows the 50 most-likely candidates through. For a simple factual query, the top 50 almost certainly include the correct answer; for creative storytelling, top-p=0.9 ensures you still get stylistic variation without the decoder dredging up tokens ranked 500th.
pitfalls: Fixed K wastes compute at high-confidence steps (model is certain, K=50 includes implausible tokens) and cuts off alternatives at low-confidence steps (model unsure, K=50 may exclude valid continuations).
Confusing top-k with 'better' or 'safer'—it simply trades diversity for avoidance of tail tokens; use top-p or temperature if you need creative output.
Setting K too small (<10) creates boilerplate repetition; too large (>200) effectively disables the filter and reintroduces rare-token blowups.
Forgetting to renormalize probabilities after filtering; the K tokens' probabilities must be rescaled to sum to 1.0 before sampling.
when_use: Use top-k as a hard cap when you want to prevent rare or nonsensical token hallucinations, especially in risk-averse applications like code generation or medical chatbots, or when combined with top-p for production inference.
when_avoid: Avoid top-k alone for creative writing, brainstorming, or open-ended tasks—use top-p with temperature instead. Do not use if you need deterministic or near-deterministic output; use greedy decoding or beam search.

faq.q: What is the difference between top-k and top-p sampling?
faq.a: Top-k uses a fixed count: pick the K highest-probability tokens. Top-p uses cumulative probability: pick tokens until their probabilities sum to p (e.g., 90%). Top-p adapts to the model's confidence; top-k does not. Both can be combined in one decoder pass.

faq.q: How do I choose the right K value?
faq.a: Start with K=40–50 for general use. Increase to 100+ for creative tasks; decrease to 20–30 for conservative, factual applications. Experiment with your task—there is no universal K.

faq.q: Does top-k sampling prevent hallucinations?
faq.a: Partially. It reduces hallucinations caused by sampling from the tail of the distribution, but it does not prevent the model from confidently generating false information ranked in the top K. Use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) or fine-tuning for stronger hallucination control.

faq.q: Can I use top-k with greedy decoding?
faq.a: No. Greedy decoding always picks the single highest-probability token; there is no sampling or distribution to restrict. Top-k only applies to sampling-based strategies.

faq.q: What happens if K is larger than the vocabulary size?
faq.a: Top-k becomes equivalent to unrestricted sampling. The decoder will consider all tokens, so the filter has no effect. Keep K well below your vocabulary size (typically 30k–100k).

title: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling
tldr: Sampling from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches threshold p, adapting to the model's confidence.
plain_english: When a model generates text, it assigns probabilities to thousands of possible next tokens. Top-p sampling says: "Keep adding tokens in order of likelihood until you've covered p% of the total probability." If the model is confident (say, 90% sure the next word is 'the'), you might use only 2–3 tokens. If it's uncertain, you might use 50+. This is like asking: "What's the smallest group of likely options that covers 90% of what the model thinks?"
how_it_works: At each generation step, the model outputs a probability distribution over all tokens. You sort tokens by probability (highest first) and cumulatively sum them until the sum exceeds p (e.g., p=0.9). Only tokens in that set can be sampled. You then sample one token uniformly from that set. Top-p is often combined with temperature (which flattens or sharpens the distribution) and a hard top-k ceiling (to prevent rare, low-probability tokens from dominating in uncertain cases). The set size changes dynamically based on the distribution shape.
why_it_matters: Top-p prevents two failure modes: setting a fixed top-k too low produces bland, repetitive text; setting it too high allows incoherent rambling. Top-p adapts automatically—on confident predictions it tightens, on uncertain ones it opens. This is why modern LLMs can be both coherent and creative without hand-tuning k for every use case.
example: OpenAI's GPT-4o uses top_p=1.0 (full distribution) by default, but users tuning for creative writing often set top_p=0.9 with temperature=1.0 to get varied but plausible responses. A customer service chatbot might use top_p=0.8 and temperature=0.5 to be consistent and on-topic. Llama models via Ollama expose the same parameter, letting you match behavior across vendors by tuning the same two knobs.
pitfalls: Setting p=1.0 is not 'maximum creativity'—it includes the entire distribution, including garbage tokens. The creative sweet spot is typically p=0.85–0.95 with temperature ≥0.7.
Top-p and temperature interact multiplicatively. Lowering temperature narrows the distribution first, making top-p act on a smaller set. Adjust one at a time during tuning.
Different model cards ship different defaults (e.g., Llama 3 vs. Mistral). Check the model card and test on your task; don't assume p=0.9 works everywhere.
Top-p doesn't prevent repetition or off-topic drift on its own. Combine it with frequency_penalty or presence_penalty for longer outputs.
when_use: Use top-p when you want creative, varied generation (creative writing, brainstorming) but need the model to adapt automatically; when you're tuning across multiple model vendors and want a consistent knob; when you don't know k in advance.
when_avoid: Avoid top-p if you need deterministic output (use greedy decoding instead). Avoid if your task requires strictly constrained vocabularies (use logit masking). Don't rely on it alone for domain-specific safety—combine with guardrails or constrained decoding.

faq.q: What's the difference between top-p and top-k sampling?
faq.a: Top-k keeps exactly k highest-probability tokens regardless of their probabilities; top-p keeps tokens until cumulative probability reaches p, adapting set size to the distribution shape. Top-p usually produces more natural text because it tightens on confident predictions.

faq.q: Why does top-p interact with temperature?
faq.a: Temperature reshapes the probability distribution before top-p is applied. Lower temperature makes the distribution more peaked, so top-p's set shrinks; higher temperature flattens it, so top-p's set grows. They compound, not replace each other.

faq.q: Is p=0.9 always the right setting?
faq.a: No. p=0.9 is a common default, but it depends on your task, model, and temperature. Creative tasks often use p=0.85–0.95; factual tasks use lower values (p=0.7–0.8). Always test on your own data.

faq.q: Should I use top-p or top-k?
faq.a: Top-p is usually better for general-purpose generation because it adapts. Use top-k only if you need strict, predictable token set sizes or have a specific reason to prefer fixed behavior.

faq.q: Does top-p prevent hallucinations?
faq.a: No. Top-p only affects sampling diversity; it doesn't validate factual correctness. Combine it with prompt engineering, retrieval augmentation, or constrained decoding to reduce hallucination.

title: Typical Sampling
tldr: Sample tokens whose information content matches the distribution's expected entropy, avoiding predictable and incoherent extremes.
plain_english: Typical sampling filters tokens by how surprising they are, keeping only those that fall within a reasonable surprise range. Think of it like choosing words that feel natural — not obvious (boring) and not bizarre (confusing). It's grounded in information theory: you measure how much information each token carries and only keep tokens near the distribution's average.
how_it_works: At each generation step, compute the negative log probability (information content) for every token in the vocabulary. Calculate the expected entropy of the full distribution. Keep only tokens whose information content falls within a threshold τ of that entropy. Sample uniformly or proportionally from this filtered set. Result: you get tokens that are typical under the model's distribution, avoiding both the highest-probability (redundant) and lowest-probability (nonsensical) tail tokens.
why_it_matters: For creative text generation, typical sampling produces more human-like variation than nucleus sampling without sacrificing coherence. It's principled — rooted in information theory rather than ad-hoc probability thresholds — making it more predictable across different model scales and domains.
example: A story-writing model using typical sampling with τ=1.0 generates: 'The detective entered the room and noticed...' with word choices that feel natural without being clichéd. Top-p sampling might skip rarer but fitting words; greedy decoding would pick the most obvious continuation every time. Typical sampling balances surprise and sense.
pitfalls: Setting τ too tight excludes useful variation; too loose reintroduces incoherence — requires empirical tuning per use case.
Entropy varies by token position and model state, so a fixed τ may behave differently early vs. late in generation.
Computational cost is higher than top-k or top-p because you calculate log-probs for the entire vocabulary.
The threshold τ is not directly comparable across models with different entropy scales.
when_use: Use typical sampling for creative writing, storytelling, or any task where you want natural variation without sacrificing coherence and where the cost of computing full-vocabulary entropy is acceptable.
when_avoid: Avoid when latency is critical, when you need deterministic output, or for tasks like code generation where you want either greedy correctness or tight nucleus sampling control.

faq.q: How is typical sampling different from top-p sampling?
faq.a: Top-p keeps cumulative probability mass below a threshold; typical sampling keeps tokens within an entropy range. Typical sampling is theory-driven and often produces different stylistic outputs, especially for creative tasks.

faq.q: What should I set the tau threshold to?
faq.a: Start with τ = 1.0 (within one unit of expected entropy) and adjust based on output quality. Smaller τ favors coherence; larger τ increases diversity. No universal default — tune empirically.

faq.q: Does typical sampling work with all model types?
faq.a: Yes, but entropy calculations assume a well-calibrated probability distribution. For quantized or distilled models, verify that log-probs are still reliable before deploying.

faq.q: Is typical sampling slower than top-p?
faq.a: Yes. Typical sampling requires computing negative log probabilities for all tokens and calculating full entropy, while top-p only needs cumulative sorting of a subset.

faq.q: Can I combine typical sampling with other decoding techniques?
faq.a: You can apply temperature before typical sampling to shift the entropy distribution. Combining with other filters (like logit bias) is possible but requires careful tuning.
